1:
The chapter opens with a king at rest in his cedar palace and an ark still housed in curtains, and the witnesses read David's unease as the mark of a devout heart. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes it a lesson in holy ambition: David could not be easy in a house of cedar while the ark of God was lodged within a tent, and his first thought in wealth and power was not 'What shall I do to fill my coffers and enlarge my dominions?' but 'What shall I do for God, to serve and honour him?' The concerns of the public, Henry says, should always lie near our hearts. He praises Nathan too for the readiness of a good minister, who no sooner heard David's design than bade him do all that was in his heart, for he had no reason to doubt that God was with him — ministers should stir up the good gifts in others as in themselves.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the setting and the chronology. The story follows the removal of the ark, though the events themselves belong to a later period of David's reign, when the LORD had given him rest from all his enemies round about, a rest he ties to 2 Samuel 7:1 and to the promise recalled at v.8; the mention of David's seed points to a time before Solomon's birth, though the exact date cannot be fixed. Ellicott notes a small stylistic fingerprint: three times in the opening verses the Chronicler writes 'David' where the parallel has 'the king' — 'the Chronicler loves the name of his ideal sovereign.' The 'house of cedars,' he adds, is the very palace Hiram's craftsmen had built for David at 1 Chronicles 14:1, its walls of Lebanon cedar; and 'under curtains' means simply in a tent, the word used in Habakkuk 3:7.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses keep the reader's eye on the larger frame. Adam Clarke lays out the chapter's shape — David consults Nathan, God answers that Solomon shall build the house, and David receives the purpose with humble joy — and, like John Wesley, refers the whole to its twin at 2 Samuel 7, 'where the same things are recorded with little variation.' Henry gathers the theological thread that will govern all that follows: what in Samuel is done 'for thy word's sake' is here done 'for thy servant's sake,' and since Christ is both the Word of God in Revelation 19:13 and the Servant of God in Isaiah 42:1, it is for his sake that the promises are made and made good to all believers — the unsearchable riches of Christ opened already in a king's wish to build a house.
2:
Nathan's whole-hearted 'Do all that is in thine heart; for God is with thee' draws the two grammatical-historical witnesses, who read the prophet's blessing both as history and as a lesson in how revelation is worded. Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as the doorway into the oracle, reproducing in one sweep the answer that follows — Nathan's approval, the word that comes that same night, the LORD's 'Thou shalt not build me an house to dwell in,' and the reminder that God had gone 'from tent to tent' and taken David 'from the sheepcote... that thou shouldest be ruler over my people Israel.' The prophet speaks first out of pious instinct; the correcting word will come only afterward.

Charles Ellicott attends to the fine differences between Chronicles and the parallel. Where the Chronicler writes simply 'Do,' Samuel has 'Go, do'; where this text says 'God,' Samuel has 'Jehovah,' though in the closing clause it speaks of the 'ark of God.' On the phrase 'all that is in thine heart' Ellicott pauses over Hebrew psychology: to the Hebrew mind the heart was the seat of the mind and the will as well as of the emotions — a conception, he remarks with some irony, more advanced than that of the great Greek Aristotle seven centuries after David, who supposed the brain to be merely a kind of cooling counterpoise to the heat of the liver. The point is not idle: Nathan is blessing not a passing mood but the settled purpose of David's whole inner man.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they mark the delicate theology of the moment. Nathan answers as a faithful minister should, encouraging a manifestly good intention and assuring the king that God is with him; yet the encouragement rests on the prophet's own judgment, not yet on a word from heaven. The verse thus stands poised on the edge of a reversal: the very design that Nathan so gladly endorses will be gently overruled before the night is out, and the reader is left to feel how even the best human counsel must wait upon the express will of God, which the next verse brings.
3:
'It came to pass the same night, that the word of God came to Nathan' — and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott seizes on the little phrase 'the same night' as a clue to the mode of the revelation. The words, he observes, indicate a dream as the method of communication, the night-vision by which God so often spoke, and he compares the terror of the nocturnal revelation in Job 4:13. It is not by an audible daylight voice but in the stillness of sleep that the LORD overtakes his prophet, and the timing is everything.

What gives the verse its weight is precisely that timing, and here Ellicott's small note joins the larger framing of Keil and Delitzsch, who in reproducing the oracle carry the reader straight from Nathan's glad approval into the corrective word that follows it. The message comes 'the same night' — that is, on the very evening of the prophet's confident 'Do all that is in thine heart.' Between the blessing and its unsaying there is scarcely time for the lamps to be put out. The God who is about to speak does not leave his servant long in a well-meant error, nor his king long in a mistaken hope.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already caught the pastoral shape of this reversal, noting how Nathan, no sooner aware of David's good design, bade him go and do it, having no reason to doubt that God was with him — and how God nonetheless prevents the performance of the very good purpose he graciously accepts. The witnesses thus let the verse do quiet but important work. Nathan had spoken truly that God was with David; he had not yet learned what God actually intended. The night word neither rebukes the king's devotion nor the prophet's encouragement, but redirects both, teaching that even a prophet's sanctioned enthusiasm must bow the moment the express word of the LORD arrives. The dream of this night overturns a good man's plan only to replace it with a far greater promise; and the reader who feels the suddenness of the correction is being prepared for the magnitude of the covenant it introduces.
4:
The heart of the night word falls in this verse — 'Thou shalt not build me an house to dwell in' — and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens both its grammar and its edge. The sentence is better rendered, he says, 'It is not thou that shalt build me the house to dwell in,' the emphasis resting on the person: not that no house shall be built, but that this is not David's task. Samuel puts it as a question implying denial, 'Wilt thou build me a house for me to dwell in?', while the Chronicler, already looking back on the famous Temple of Solomon, writes pointedly of 'the house' — the one great house every later reader would know. Ellicott notes too the tender address that opens the oracle, 'David my servant,' where Samuel has the fuller 'unto my servant, unto David.'

Keil and Delitzsch, reproducing the oracle, set this refusal within its whole movement: the LORD who declines David's offer of a cedar house is the same who took the shepherd from the fold and made him ruler, and who will presently turn the whole transaction around. The prohibition, in their reading, is not a rebuff but the hinge of a reversal.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had drawn the pastoral sense out at length. David must not build this house; he must prepare for it, but not do it — as Moses might bring Israel within sight of Canaan yet leave it to Joshua to put them in possession. It is the prerogative of Christ, Henry adds, to be both the author and finisher of his work; and no one man should think to engross every good work and bring it to perfection — let something be left for those who succeed. Yet the refusal carries no reproach: David's preferment was not in vain, his good purpose not wasted, for God would recompense the willing mind as fully as the finished deed. The witnesses agree that the 'No' of this verse is spoken in love, a checking of the king's zeal that is at once the seedbed of a promise immeasurably larger than the house he had hoped to raise.
5:
'I have not dwelt in an house since the day that I brought up Israel unto this day; but have gone from tent to tent' — the witnesses trace the LORD's own long history of homelessness among his people. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, following the Targum and Jarchi, fills in the itinerary of the wandering sanctuary: 'I have transferred my tabernacle from Gilgal to Nob, from Nob to Shiloh, and from Shiloh to Gibeon.' The God of Israel had been a pilgrim God, moving with a moving people, content in a curtained tent through all the generations from the Exodus down to David's own day.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works closely over the Hebrew, which he finds compressed almost past sense. Literally the clause reads 'and I became from tent to tent, and from dwelling' — a phrase so brief, he judges, that some words must have dropped out, so that either the fuller reading of Samuel is original here or the sense must be restored as 'I continued walking in a tent and in a dwelling,' or better still, 'I walked from tent to tent, and from dwelling to dwelling.' The allusion, he suggests, is to the various sanctuaries anciently recognised — Bethel, Mizpeh, Shiloh — at which the ark had rested in turn through the age of the judges. He observes that the word rendered 'tabernacle,' mishkan, is a more general term than 'tent,' taking in the sacred tent together with its surrounding court.

The two witnesses do not divide, and their labours converge on a single theology. The LORD's centuries-long acceptance of a tent is not a token of poverty or neglect but of sovereign freedom: he had never once murmured at the curtains, never demanded cedar walls, never bound his presence to a fixed and settled house. Clarke's roll of resting-places and Ellicott's patient grammar meet in the same conclusion — that a God who has willingly walked with his people from Gilgal to Gibeon cannot be laid under obligation by any structure David might raise. Whatever house is one day to be built will be built not to supply a lack in God, who needed none, but at his own gracious initiative and in his own appointed time; and that reversal, from David's offer to God's promise, is precisely where the oracle is heading.
6:
The oracle presses its point with a rhetorical question — in all his walking with Israel, had the LORD ever once asked any of the judges, 'Why have ye not built me an house of cedars?' — and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the verse phrase by phrase. 'Wheresoever' he renders 'as long as,' or literally 'in all that'; 'with all Israel' answers to Samuel's 'among all the sons of Israel.' The most interesting difference is that where this text reads 'the judges of Israel,' Samuel has 'tribes' — a word, Ellicott notes, that would be less intelligible in later times and has probably been replaced by the plainer 'judges,' though the two are not really incompatible, since the ascendancy of different tribes at different epochs is bound up with the individual rulers God raised. The verb 'to feed,' he adds, means to shepherd or tend, that is, to govern.

The force of the question is that God never once laid the building of a house upon those he appointed to shepherd his people; the omission was deliberate and eloquent. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had drawn out the same lesson in his survey of the chapter: God's ark was content with a tabernacle, and he never so much as mentioned the building of a house for it — not even when he had settled his people in great and goodly cities they had not built. He had commanded the judges to feed his people, but never bidden them build him a house. We may well be content, Henry concludes, for a while with mean accommodations, since God's ark was so.

The witnesses agree in reading the verse as the divine disproof of any charge of neglect. Israel's leaders had not failed in a duty God imposed, for he had imposed none; the LORD had asked to be given a flock to feed, not a house of cedar to inhabit. And this becomes the ground of the great reversal to come: the God who never demanded a house from his shepherd-rulers is under no debt to David, and is therefore wholly free to give, rather than to receive, the house that this chapter will make everlasting.
7:
'Now therefore thus shalt thou say unto my servant David... I took thee from the sheepcote, even from following the sheep, that thou shouldest be ruler over my people Israel.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the words a deliberate stress on the divine initiative: the pronoun is emphatic — 'I it was who took thee from the pasture' — so that every step of David's rise is traced back to God's own hand. 'From following' is literally 'from behind' the flock, and the title 'ruler' renders the Hebrew nagid, the word used elsewhere in Chronicles for the leader God designates. Ellicott points to the twin celebration of this call in Psalms 78:70-72, where the LORD takes David from the sheepfolds to shepherd Jacob his people.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had made this verse the answer to a wounded thought. Lest David imagine that being forbidden the temple left his preferment empty, God reminds him of the height from which he was raised: 'I took thee from the sheep-cote, though not to be a builder of the temple, yet to be ruler over my people Israel; that is honour enough for thee; leave the other to one that shall come after thee.' Why should one man think to engross all the business and bring every good work to perfection? Let something be reserved for those who follow. The prohibition, so read, is no demotion but a right dividing of labour: the shepherd-king is given a shepherd's crown, and the temple is left to another hand.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott's grammar and Henry's pastoral application meet in a single confession — that David is wholly God's making. He did not climb to the throne; he was lifted to it from behind the sheep. And this reminder is exactly calculated to prepare him for the promise ahead: the man who owes his whole greatness to a God who 'took' him has no ground to grieve at being told he shall not build, and every ground to marvel at being told that God will build for him instead. The verse humbles in order to exalt, tracing the king back to the fold so that the covenant may come to him purely as gift.
8:
'And I have been with thee whithersoever thou hast walked, and have cut off all thine enemies from before thee, and have made thee a name.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse as a summary of David's whole career under the guardianship of God. 'Whithersoever thou hast walked' is the same broad phrase used just above — throughout thy entire course. The cutting off of 'all thine enemies' refers, he thinks, not merely to the death of Saul and the overthrow of his house but to the successful conclusion of several of the wars recorded in the following chapters. And where the English reads 'and have made thee a name,' the Hebrew is better taken as a promise still opening — 'and I will make thee a great name, like the name of the great men that are in the earth' — the sovereigns, Ellicott specifies, of Egypt and Babylon, of Tyre, and of the Hittite states.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the same clauses into the logic of David's calling. God had given him victories and made him a name, and further intended by him to establish his people Israel and secure them against their enemies; that, Henry says, must be the work of a man of war, fit for it, while the building of the house is left to one never cut out for a soldier. The martial vocation and the temple vocation are distinct gifts, and God distributes them as he pleases.

The witnesses converge on the tender arithmetic of grace running through the oracle: presence — 'I have been with thee'; protection — 'I have cut off all thine enemies'; and reputation — 'I will make thee a name.' Each is God's doing, not David's earning. And the recital is not flattery but foundation: having rehearsed all he has already freely done, the LORD is about to promise what surpasses it all. The God who gave the shepherd victories and a name is the God who will give his house a throne that outlasts every name among the great men of the earth, so that the whole retrospect bends forward toward the covenant word now at hand.
9:
The promise widens from the king to the whole nation — 'I will ordain a place for my people Israel, and will plant them, that they may dwell in their place, and be moved no more' — and the witnesses read it as a pledge of settled rest after long insecurity. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott compares the language of planting to the song of Exodus 15:17 and to Psalms 44:2-3, where God plants his people in the land by driving out the nations. The promise had real point, he observes, for although Israel had effected a settlement in Canaan, the history shows that down to David's time the tribal boundaries fluctuated greatly and the inroads of surrounding peoples made their tenure very uncertain. 'Children of wickedness' he glosses as simply wicked men, on the model of 'sons of Belial'; 'waste them' carries an Aramaic sense of the verb, where Samuel's 'afflict them' seems the more original; and 'as at the beginning' looks back to the bondage in Egypt.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on that backward glance. 'Neither shall the children of wickedness' waste them, he explains, means that Israel shall no more be brought into servitude as they were in the days when they sojourned in Egypt — the old slavery shall not return. The promise is thus measured against the very worst the nation had known: not merely peace, but security against a repetition of Egypt.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott's history of shifting borders and Clarke's memory of the house of bondage meet in one assurance — that the God who is founding an everlasting dynasty is at the same time founding a settled people for it to rule. The covenant with the house of David is not a private favour to a family but the guarantee of a home for Israel, planted and no more uprooted. And the promise reaches past its immediate horizon: the rest here pledged, only partly enjoyed under Solomon and often broken afterward, points forward to the unshakable security that belongs to the kingdom of David's greater Son, in which the planting of God's people is at last made permanent.
10:
The oracle now turns on itself with a stroke of holy irony, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott savours the reversal. After recalling the troubled days 'since the time that I commanded judges over my people' and renewing the pledge 'I will subdue all thine enemies,' the LORD adds, literally, 'And I have told thee, and a house will Jehovah build thee.' Here, Ellicott says, is 'a sort of ironical inversion of David's wish to build a house for the Lord': the king had proposed to raise God a house of cedar, and God answers that he will instead raise the king a house. The term is figurative — 'house' in the sense of offspring, a dynasty, as in Psalms 127:1 — and the Chronicler, he notes, deliberately writes 'build' rather than Samuel's 'make,' with an eye to the play on the Hebrew words banah, to build, and banim, sons. The house David cannot build for God, God will build out of David's own sons.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the consolation the inversion carries: must David think his purpose was in vain, and that he should lose the reward of it? No — it being God's own act that prevented the execution of it, he shall be as fully recompensed as if the work had been done. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry says the same in his survey: 'The Lord will build thee a house, and annex the crown of Israel to it'; if there be a willing mind, it shall not only be accepted but thus richly rewarded.

The witnesses do not divide over the wordplay; they delight in it together. The verse is the pivot of the whole chapter, the moment at which the direction of grace is reversed. David reaches up to give, and finds his hands filled instead; he offers God a dwelling, and is promised a dynasty. And the witnesses feel that the promised 'house' will not be exhausted by Solomon's line: the same divine builder who founds David's family will, in the fullness of time, build the living house of which the temple was only a shadow, so that the pun on stone and sons opens at last onto the everlasting kingdom the following verses unfold.
11:
The covenant proper now begins — 'when thy days be expired that thou must go to be with thy fathers, I will raise up thy seed after thee, which shall be of thy sons; and I will establish his kingdom' — and the two grammatical-historical witnesses probe both its wording and a real difficulty beneath it. Keil and Delitzsch note that the phrase 'to go with thy fathers,' used of dying, resembles the idiom 'to go the way of all the world' in 1 Kings 2:2 and is actually more primitive than the commoner 'to lie down with thy fathers' of 2 Samuel 7:12. Charles Ellicott agrees that 'be expired' means 'are fulfilled,' and that 'which shall be of thy sons' stands where Samuel has the more vivid 'which shall go forth from thy bowels.'

Here, however, the witnesses genuinely divide over the character of the change. Ellicott holds that the Chronicler has paraphrased Samuel's older and more physical expression 'to suit the taste of a later age,' and points to 'his kingdom,' malkuth, as a later word than Samuel's synonym — a case, in his view, of deliberate literary refinement. Keil and Delitzsch resist that account. The phrase 'of thy sons,' they insist, need not be altered to match Samuel, nor should it be taken, with Bertheau, as an alteration the Chronicler made to escape a difficulty — namely, that here only the birth of Solomon is promised, whereas Nathan's speech was delivered when David already had rest from his enemies, as at 2 Samuel 8:1, and so, on the usual view, after Solomon was already born. The supposed difficulty, they answer, had already been removed by an omission earlier in the chapter, so there was no motive for such a change.

So the debate is a small but characteristic one: was the Chronicler smoothing and modernizing an older text, as Ellicott and Bertheau suppose, or is his wording simply an independent and even primitive form of the tradition, wrongly suspected of harmonizing tampering, as Keil and Delitzsch contend? Beneath the philology both witnesses hold the substance in common. God pledges to David a seed and an established kingdom that will outlast the king's own death; the promise leaps the grave, so that what David could not finish in his lifetime God will raise up after him. The 'seed' begins in Solomon, but the language of an established kingdom already strains toward something more enduring than any single son, as the next verses will make explicit.
12:
'He shall build me an house, and I will stablish his throne for ever' — and with the word 'for ever' the promise begins visibly to outgrow its first object. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the Hebrew emphasis: 'He' is the emphatic word — this son, and not David, is the appointed builder. Where Samuel says the son shall build 'for my name,' Chronicles says simply 'build me'; and Samuel's 'throne of his kingdom' is here abridged to 'his throne.' Keil and Delitzsch, reproducing the oracle through to David's answering prayer, let the verse stand as the second panel of the promise: the son will do what the father may not, and the throne so founded is pledged an endless duration.

It is that endless duration which the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes as the key to the whole. 'I will establish his throne for ever' — 'David was a type of Christ,' he writes, 'and concerning him the prophecy is literally true,' pointing to the great oracle of Isaiah 9:7, where the government and peace of the child born to David's throne have no end. Solomon builds a house of stone and reigns his span; but a throne 'for ever' can be literally true only of the greater Son whom Solomon prefigures.

The witnesses do not so much divide as read on different planes at once, and the tension is fruitful. To the grammarian's eye the immediate referent is plainly Solomon, the son soon to build the Temple; to the theologian's eye the word 'for ever' bursts that frame and can rest finally only on Christ. Chronicles itself, by dropping the conditional clause that Samuel attaches, seems to lean toward the larger reading, as the following verse will show. The verse thus holds the two builders together — the son who raises the temple of cedar and gold, and the Son who raises the everlasting house of God — and refuses to let the reader stop at the first. What begins as a promise about Solomon's masonry ends as a promise about an eternal throne, and the distance between the two is the space the gospel will one day fill.
13:
'I will be his father, and he shall be my son: and I will not take my mercy away from him, as I took it from him that was before thee.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening with its full mutuality — 'I, on my part, will become a father unto him, and he, on his part, shall become a son to me' — and hears behind it the royal adoption of Psalms 2:7. But his sharpest observation concerns what Chronicles leaves out. After these words Samuel adds a solemn condition: 'If he commit iniquity I will chasten him with the rod of men, and with the stripes of the children of men.' The omission here, Ellicott judges, 'is probably not a mere abridgment.' The prophecy looks beyond Solomon to Him of whom the greatest princes of David's house were but imperfect types; and while the warning of chastisement was amply fulfilled in Solomon and his successors, it could not apply to the true Anointed of Jehovah, and is therefore suppressed as a merely transitory element in the promise.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the pledge of unfailing mercy against the memory of the last dynasty: 'I will not take my mercy away from him' means 'I will not cut off his family from the throne, as I did that of his predecessor Saul.' The clause 'him that was before thee,' both witnesses agree, is Saul, whose house God stripped of the kingdom; David's house is promised the very mercy Saul's forfeited.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they show how the Chronicler's very silence preaches. By dropping the threat of the rod, the text lifts the reader's eye from the fallible sons who needed chastening to the faultless Son who never would; the father-son bond that begins as God's covenant with Solomon becomes the language in which Scripture will speak of the eternal Sonship. Clarke's contrast with Saul secures the historical ground — this is a real, unbreakable pledge to a real dynasty — while Ellicott's account of the omission opens the window toward Christ. The mercy that will never depart from David's house is, in the end, the mercy that rests without measure on the one Son to whom the whole promise runs.
14:
The promise reaches its summit — 'But I will settle him in mine house and in my kingdom for ever: and his throne shall be established for evermore' — and the witnesses fasten on the striking change of persons the Chronicler has introduced. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that 'settle him' is literally 'make him stand,' and that where Samuel had spoken of 'thine house and thy kingdom' being maintained before David, Chronicles says 'mine house and my kingdom' — God's house, God's kingdom. The change, Ellicott explains, 'brings out more clearly the theocratic nature of the Davidic kingdom': Solomon and his successors were to reign not as owners but as vicegerents of Jehovah, standing in a kingdom that was always his.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes that theocratic note and follows it all the way to Christ. To be set in God's house and God's kingdom 'agrees but imperfectly with Solomon, or his successors, but strictly and properly with Christ, to whom alone that promise also of an everlasting establishment in this kingdom belongs.' The 'kingdom,' he adds, is God's kingdom 'in a large and general sense,' and this too belongs singularly to the Messiah, who was to be king not of Israel only but of all nations — an intimation, more fully revealed in the New Testament, that Christ is the head of the whole church of Jews and Gentiles, and indeed of all creatures. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had said the same in his overview: David must not confine his thoughts to the temporal prosperity of his family, but entertain himself with the prospect of the kingdom of the Messiah, who should descend from his loins and whose throne should be established for evermore.

Here the witnesses stand wholly together, and the double reading that ran through the earlier verses resolves into one confession. Solomon is set in the house and kingdom for his day; but 'for ever' and 'for evermore' can terminate only on Christ, in whom alone the Davidic throne is made eternal. Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the oracle straight on to David's prayer, let the words stand as the climax of Nathan's message: the shepherd's dynasty is not merely to endure a few generations but to issue at last in an everlasting kingdom — the very kingdom the king will now bow down to receive with wonder.
15:
'According to all these words, and according to all this vision, so did Nathan speak unto David' — a verse of transition, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott uses it to reflect on the nature of the record itself. The matter of the prophecy, he affirms, undoubtedly rests upon authentic tradition; but neither the compiler of Samuel nor the Chronicler professes to give an exact, verbatim report of Nathan's words, 'as if they had been taken down on the spot... by some shorthand reporter.' The modern demand for literal accuracy, Ellicott observes, was unknown to Oriental antiquity. This very verse, in naming 'all these words' and 'all this vision,' seems rather to imply that the essence than the precise wording of the oracle has been preserved — which is why, where the two narratives vary, sometimes Samuel and sometimes Chronicles keeps the more original form.

That candid observation prepares the reader for the long prayer that follows, and Ellicott turns to it directly: from here the chapter passes to David's response, which the same principle governs. The prayer, he grants, may be an 'ideal soliloquy' rather than a stenographic transcript, yet it 'undoubtedly breathes the genuine Davidic spirit' — and why, he asks, may not David himself have recorded the substance of it as a memorial of so great a night?

Brief as it is, the verse marks a real hinge in the chapter, and the single witness lets its two functions show. Backward, it closes the oracle: Nathan withholds nothing, but delivers the whole vision faithfully, word and substance alike, so that the promise reaches David exactly as God gave it. Forward, it opens the prayer: having heard 'all these words,' David will not argue or bargain but adore. Ellicott's reminder that we possess the essence rather than a transcript is no diminishment of the passage but a fitting frame for it, teaching the reader to weigh the truth conveyed above the tally of syllables — and the truth conveyed is a covenant that has just made a shepherd's house eternal, to which the only fit answer is the worship now beginning.
16:
David 'came and sat before the LORD' and answered the promise with worship, and the witnesses gather warmly around the king's astonished humility. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the scene: David sat 'before the LORD' in the tent that housed the ark, and his opening 'Who am I, O LORD God, and what is mine house, that thou hast brought me hitherto?' uses the shorter form of the pronoun and the name 'Jehovah Elohim,' where Samuel's 'Adonai Jehovah' — 'my Lord, Jehovah' — is the more original; 'mine house' means his family, and 'hitherto' means to this pitch of greatness. He compares the whole outpouring with David's last words in 2 Samuel 23:5. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke catches the concrete force of the question: 'I am not of any regal family, and have no natural right to the throne' — the shepherd marvels that a crown, and now a covenant, should light on him at all.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the prayer as a model of believing devotion — how humbly David abases himself, how highly he advances the name of God, with what assurance he builds upon the promise and puts it in suit. Henry notices what Chronicles adds to the Samuel account: where Samuel asks by way of question, 'Is this the manner of men, O Lord God?', Chronicles turns it to acknowledgment — 'Thou hast regarded me according to the estate of a man of high degree.' God, by the covenant-relations into which he admits believers, the titles he gives them and the favours he prepares for them, regards them as men of high degree though they are in themselves mean and vile.

But Henry presses further, to a reading that reaches to Christ. Some, he notes, render the words otherwise: 'Thou hast looked upon me in the form of a man who art in the highest, the Lord God,' and so the verse 'points at the Messiah.' As Abraham saw Christ's day and was glad, so David saw it by faith, 'saw it in fashion as a man, the Word made flesh, and yet saw his glory as that of the only-begotten of the Father.' Nor should it seem strange, Henry adds, that David should speak so plainly of the two natures of Christ, who in spirit called him Lord though he knew he was to be his Son in Psalms 110:1, and foresaw him made for a little while lower than the angels, then crowned with glory and honour, as in Hebrews 2:6-7. John Wesley echoes the whole: how humbly David abases himself, how highly he magnifies the God of Israel, what an example of believing, fervent prayer. Keil and Delitzsch, reproducing the prayer, simply carry the words forward; the witnesses do not divide, but stand together before a king who, offered an everlasting house, can only ask who he is that God should have brought him so far.
17:
'And yet this was a small thing in thine eyes, O God; for thou hast also spoken of thy servant's house for a great while to come.' David marvels that his unlooked-for exaltation was, in God's sight, not enough — the LORD has gone on to reveal the far future of his offspring. The verse then turns on a clause the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott frankly calls obscure: 'hast regarded me according to the estate of a man of high degree,' where Samuel has simply 'and this is the law of man, O Lord God.'

Ellicott lays the difficulty open and offers more than one way through it, so that the verse becomes a small forum of competing readings. The word Samuel uses, torah, strictly means teaching, as when the prophet's oracles are called torah in Isaiah 8:16; with a slight adjustment of the text one may then translate, 'and thou regardest me' — the same verb rendered in Luke 1:48 — 'like man's teaching that bringeth up,' the instruction a parent gives a child, as in Psalms 32:8 and Ezekiel 19:2. On this reading God's very revelation is part of David's moral discipline: the prohibition of the Temple was a check, but the promise attached to it was the encouragement of a heavenly Teacher. If, however, the other sense of torah be allowed, the words may mean 'thou regardest me after the manner of men that exalteth' — as human benefactors help forward those they favour. The old versions, Ellicott admits, give no help in deciding.

Against these philological options the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had set a frankly theological one, reading the same difficult clause as a glimpse of the Messiah — 'Thou hast made me to see, according to the form of a man, the majesty of the Lord God,' David beholding by faith the Word made flesh. So the verse carries a genuine debate: is the phrase a lesson drawn from the way God teaches or the way great men advance their favourites, or is it a prophetic sight of Christ in the form of a man? The witnesses do not force a decision, and the reader is left with the very wonder David voices — that whatever the exact rendering, God has stooped to deal with a shepherd 'for a great while to come,' making his little house the theme of an everlasting purpose.
18:
'What can David speak more to thee for the honour of thy servant? for thou knowest thy servant.' The king's words falter under the weight of grace, and the witnesses read the faltering itself as eloquence. Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the prayer forward, let the verse open the movement in which David confesses that language has run out before God's goodness — there is nothing left to say, for God already knows his servant through and through. Reading on into the verses that follow, they hear the same note swell — 'for thy servant's sake... hast thou done all this greatness' — so that David's inability to speak more is not empty silence but the overflow of a heart that has watched God act beyond all its words.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works at the difficult Hebrew. Samuel here has a word for 'speak' that Chronicles seems to have lost, and the term rendered 'for the honour,' Ellicott suggests, may itself be a corruption of that verb 'to speak' — so that the sense may originally have run, 'What can David say more unto thee?' The following phrase 'of thy servant,' standing in the accusative, he takes to be a mistaken repetition of the same word at the end of the verse. The textual tangle, however, does not obscure the drift, which is David's sense that praise has reached its limit.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the lesson the verse offers plainly: 'the honour God puts upon his servants, by taking them into covenant and communion with himself, is so great that they need not, they cannot desire to be more highly honoured.' The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had made the identical point in surveying the prayer — were believers to sit down and wish, they could not speak more for their own honour than the word of God has already spoken over them. The witnesses do not divide. Whether the verse is read through Ellicott's careful emendations or Wesley's and Henry's devotion, it comes to the same confession: God knows his servant, and has honoured him past all asking, so that the only fit response is the speechless wonder David here expresses. To be taken into covenant with God is an exaltation beyond which desire itself has nothing to add.
19:
'O LORD, for thy servant's sake, and according to thine own heart, hast thou done all this greatness, in making known all these great things.' The clause 'for thy servant's sake' becomes the occasion of a genuine divergence, for the parallel in Samuel reads 'for thy word's sake,' and the witnesses weigh the difference in opposite ways.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott treats it as a matter of text and transmission. Samuel's 'for thy word's sake' is 'the more original,' he judges; 'heart' here means purpose or intent; and the doubled expression 'greatness... greatnesses' is 'probably a scribe's error,' Samuel preserving the right text, 'thou hast done all this greatness' — a work of power shown precisely in God's making known beforehand what shall come to pass. That miracle of prediction, Ellicott adds, is the very thing Isaiah makes the mark of the true God over against the idols, who can foretell nothing, in Isaiah 41:21. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley simply records the variant, noting that at the corresponding place in 2 Samuel 7:21 it stands 'for thy word's sake,' for the sake of the promise God had made.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, however, refuses to let the two readings simply cancel, and finds a treasure in their difference. What Samuel calls 'for thy word's sake' Chronicles calls 'for thy servant's sake'; and since Jesus Christ is both the Word of God in Revelation 19:13 and the Servant of God in Isaiah 42:1, it is 'for his sake, upon the score of his mediation, that the promises are both made and made good to all believers; it is in him that they are yea and amen.' The two texts together, on Henry's reading, spell out the one Mediator.

So the verse holds a real debate about the variant wording: is the change from 'word' to 'servant' a textual shift to be adjudicated in favour of the older reading, as Ellicott and Wesley take it, or a providential doubling in which the Word and the Servant are seen to be the same Christ, as Henry finds? The witnesses do not resolve it, and both instincts have their worth — the one guarding the text, the other mining its depths. What neither doubts is David's own point: that all this greatness flows from God's free heart and God's faithful word, made known to a servant who had done nothing to deserve it.
20:
'O LORD, there is none like thee, neither is there any God beside thee, according to all that we have heard with our ears.' David's wonder at God's covenant opens outward into adoration of God's uniqueness, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the Chronicler's compression has cost a word or two here. The fuller confession stands in the parallel at 2 Samuel 7:22 — 'Wherefore thou art great, O LORD God: for there is none like thee' — where the incomparable greatness is stated before the incomparable uniqueness. Keil and Delitzsch, reproducing the prayer, let the verse stand as the theological centre of David's praise: from the particular mercy shown to his house the king rises to the universal truth that there is no God but this one.

The witnesses agree that the ground of the confession is revelation, not speculation — 'according to all that we have heard with our ears.' David does not reason his way to the sole deity of the LORD; he confesses what Israel has been taught by God's own acts and words, handed down and heard. The uniqueness of God is known because God has made himself known, in the Exodus, in the gift of the land, and now in this very promise to a shepherd's house.

Brief as the verse is, its placement is telling, and the single sustained note the witnesses draw from it is the link between covenant and confession. It is precisely the God who stoops to bind himself to David who is here owned to have no equal and no rival; the intimacy of the promise and the transcendence of the Promiser are held in one breath. A lesser god might flatter a king; only the true God, beside whom there is none other, could pledge an everlasting throne and be believed. The very compression Ellicott notes, which has dropped a word from the fuller confession of Samuel, leaves the incomparability all the more stark and unadorned. So David's thanksgiving becomes doxology, and the mercy to his house becomes a window onto the sole deity of the LORD — the same movement from gift to Giver that the next verses carry on, from God's uniqueness to the uniqueness of the people he redeemed.
21:
From the uniqueness of God David turns to the uniqueness of God's people — 'And what one nation in the earth is like thy people Israel, whom God went to redeem to be his own people?' — and the two grammatical-historical witnesses read the verse as the counterpart of the one before. Charles Ellicott renders it, 'And who is like thy people Israel, a single, isolated race on the earth?', comparing the picture of a people dwelling alone in Numbers 23:9. He distinguishes the two words the verse uses: 'nation,' goy, means a race united by common blood, speech, and country, while 'people,' am, means a political community owning one sovereign. God 'went' — literally marched — to redeem this people to himself, 'to make thee a name of greatness and terribleness,' both nouns being plural and implying renown for great and terrible deeds, won by driving out the nations before them.

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the same phrase, noting how Chronicles binds 'great and fearful deeds' directly to the making of a name, where Samuel spells it out more fully with an added clause about God's doing great and terrible things for his people; the Chronicler's tighter construction, they hold, is no proof that anything has been lost from his text. Ellicott adds a revealing point of comparison: Samuel's account speaks of God driving out 'nations and their gods,' but Chronicles omits 'his gods.' The Chronicler, he suggests — like Isaiah, and unlike the older writers who could speak of foreign gods as real though subordinate beings — regarded the heathen deities as sheer non-entities, and so left them out.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rounds out David's doxology with a matched pair of incomparables: as there is no God like the LORD, so there is no nation like the people he redeemed. The redemption in view is the Exodus, God marching out to make a slave-people his own and to win himself a name among the nations by their deliverance; and the whole recollection serves David's prayer, for the God who once redeemed a nation to himself for ever is the God now pledging an everlasting house to that nation's king. The greatness of the past redemption is the pledge of the greatness of the promise, and Israel's singularity among the peoples becomes the setting of the covenant David has just received.
22:
'For thy people Israel didst thou make thine own people for ever; and thou, LORD, becamest their God.' The verse states the covenant bond in its two directions at once, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders each side with care. The first clause is literally 'And thou gavest thy people Israel unto thyself for a people' — where Samuel has 'confirmedst it,' a difference Ellicott traces to a partial obliteration of the text. The second clause, 'and thou, Jehovah, becamest unto them for a God,' he anchors in the classic covenant formula running from Genesis 17:7-8 onward, the pledge by which the LORD takes a people to be his and gives himself to be their God.

Keil and Delitzsch, reproducing this closing section of the prayer, let the verse stand as the ground of the petitions that follow: because God has already made Israel his own for ever and become their God, David can dare to ask that the further word about his house be established. The 'for ever' of the national covenant underwrites the 'for ever' of the dynastic one.

Though only the two grammatical witnesses attend closely here, the verse carries the whole logic of David's confidence. He is not asking God to begin something new but to confirm what God's own character has already set in motion. The bond between the LORD and Israel is not a bargain that may lapse but a gift 'for ever,' sealed by the mutual formula — his people, their God. That same formula, as Ellicott's cross-references show, echoes through the patriarchal promises and the deliverance from Egypt — 'I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God,' in Exodus 6:7 — so that David, in praying, is standing on the oldest ground in Israel's faith. And it is on that unbreakable footing that the prayer will now turn from praise to plea: the God who bound himself to a nation for ever may surely be trusted to make good his word to that nation's house, so that the covenant recited in this verse becomes the very warrant for the requests of the next.
23:
With this verse David's prayer turns from adoration to petition — 'Therefore now, LORD, let the thing that thou hast spoken concerning thy servant and concerning his house be established for ever, and do as thou hast said.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the small textual details: where Chronicles addresses simply 'LORD,' Samuel adds 'God'; and the request 'let the thing be established' means, more exactly, let the word — the promise God has spoken — be upheld, maintained, made sure, Samuel using a different verb, 'establish thou.' The prayer, in other words, does no more than ask God to be true to his own pledge.

Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the closing section of the prayer as a single sweep, let this petition stand at its head: having rehearsed God's uniqueness, Israel's redemption, and the covenant bond, David now lays hold of the specific promise about his house and asks that it be made to stand for ever. The plea rests entirely on the word already given; David asks for nothing God has not first offered.

Brief as the genuine comment is, the verse is a model of believing prayer, and the witnesses let its structure teach. David does not invent a request or press his own wishes; he takes the very words of the oracle — 'thy servant,' 'his house,' 'for ever' — and hands them back to God as a petition. 'Do as thou hast said' is the whole of it. The doubled plea, 'let the thing be established... and do as thou hast said,' presses the one request from both sides — that God would uphold his word and then enact it — and Ellicott's careful distinction of the two verbs only underlines how wholly the petition leans on the promise rather than on any merit in the asker. This is the boldness Scripture commends: to turn God's promises into prayers, holding him to what he has freely spoken. And because the thing prayed for is the thing God has purposed, the petition is as good as granted before it is finished — a confidence that will break into open assurance by the time the prayer reaches its close.
24:
'Let it even be established, that thy name may be magnified for ever, saying, The LORD of hosts is the God of Israel, even a God to Israel: and let the house of David thy servant be established before thee.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the emphatic repetition 'let it even be established' — 'Yea, let it be assured' — is wanting in Samuel, and that the confession at its heart runs, 'Jehovah Sabaoth, God of Israel, is God to Israel.' The closing clause about the house of David, he adds, has no verb in the Hebrew and might even be read as part of what men will say in praise hereafter: 'The house of David thy servant is established before thee.'

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on the little phrase 'a God to Israel' and draws out its force: God 'is really to his people that which he hath styled himself, their God, having taken such care of them, and shewed such mercy and truth to them, as fully answered that title.' The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had pressed exactly this distinction in his survey of the prayer. To be called 'the God of Israel' is to bear the name; to be 'a God to Israel' is to answer to it — to fill up the relation and do for them all that the name promises. There were gods 'of' Assyria and Egypt, of Hamath and Arpad, Henry observes, but they were no gods 'to' those nations, standing them in no stead, mere ciphers and nothing but a name; whereas the God of Israel is a God to Israel, all his perfections redounding to their real benefit.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott's grammar and Wesley's and Henry's devotion meet in a single insight: David asks that the covenant be made sure not chiefly for his own glory but that God's name may be magnified for ever, vindicated before the nations as a God who is genuinely, actively God to his people. The establishing of David's house and the magnifying of God's name are, in the king's prayer, one petition — for the standing of the dynasty is to be the visible proof that the LORD of hosts truly answers to the title he has taken, and is to Israel all that his name declares.
25:
David now names the very ground of his boldness — 'For thou, O my God, hast told thy servant that thou wilt build him an house: therefore thy servant hath found in his heart to pray before thee.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the first clause with a vivid Hebrew idiom: God has 'uncovered the ear' of his servant, whispering to him the purpose to build him a house — the same phrase used of God's private disclosure to Samuel in 1 Samuel 9:15. The second clause, 'hath found in his heart to pray,' is more exactly 'hath found his heart,' a unique expression Ellicott takes to mean 'hath taken courage'; Samuel spells the noun out and adds that David prayed 'this prayer.'

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reaches the same sense by way of the Targum, which gives a fuller turn: 'Therefore thy servant hath found an opening of mouth, that he might pray before thee.' The revelation of God's intention is what unstops the servant's lips; had God not first spoken, David would have had no warrant to ask.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they lay bare the logic of all true prayer. David's petition is not a venture of presumption but a response to disclosure: because God has 'uncovered his ear' with a promise, David has 'found his heart' to pray. The order is everything — God speaks first, and only then does the servant dare to answer. Whether one takes Ellicott's 'taken courage' or Clarke's 'opening of mouth,' the point is the same: prayer here is the echo of promise, boldness born not of the worshipper's worth but of the word he has heard. It is the pattern of every believing prayer that follows a promise — the ear first uncovered, the heart then found, the mouth at last opened. David asks for a house only because God has said he will build one; and that is why his asking is so confident, for he is not wringing a gift from a reluctant God but returning to God the very purpose God has revealed. The courage to pray, the witnesses agree, is itself a gift, kindled by the God who first uncovers the ear.
26:
'And now, LORD, thou art God, and hast promised this goodness unto thy servant.' The petition rests back upon the character of the Promiser, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the confession with its full weight: 'Jehovah, thou art the God' — the one true God — over against every pretended deity. He notes that the Chronicler here omits a clause that stands in the parallel: Samuel adds 'and thy words become truth,' that is, prove true. Whether by abridgment or by the accidents of transmission, Chronicles lets the bare confession stand — God is God, and he has promised.

Keil and Delitzsch, reproducing the close of the prayer, let the verse serve as the pivot between plea and expected blessing: David has asked that the word be established, and now grounds that request in the two facts that leave no room for doubt — that the LORD is truly God, and that this same God has freely promised 'this goodness' to his servant. Deity and promise together make the outcome certain.

Short as it is, the verse gathers the sinew of David's confidence, and the witnesses let its two clauses stand as the pillars of faith. Because the LORD is God, he is able to perform what he has spoken; because he has promised, he is willing. The one guards against doubting God's power, the other against doubting his goodwill. Even the clause Chronicles omits — that God's words 'become truth' — is implied in the two it keeps, for a God who is truly God cannot promise and fail. The title 'thou art God' is no bare metaphysical claim but the covenant confession of the whole prayer: the same LORD who redeemed Israel and lifted David from the fold now stands behind the promise, so that his Godhead and his faithfulness are one and the same guarantee. So David's prayer, having begun in astonished 'Who am I?', arrives at solid ground: not his own worth, which is nothing, but the deity and the pledged goodness of God, which are everything. On that footing the final petition, and its bold assurance, can at last be spoken.
27:
The prayer ends where the promise began, on the word 'for ever' — 'Now therefore let it please thee to bless the house of thy servant, that it may be before thee for ever: for thou blessest, O LORD, and it shall be blessed for ever.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the closing confidence in the Hebrew. 'Let it please thee to bless' is better rendered 'and now thou hast willed to bless,' where Samuel has the plainer imperative 'be willing, and bless'; and 'for thou blessest, O LORD' is more exactly 'for thou, Jehovah, hast blessed' — a past act on which the future rests. Samuel's version is fuller still, spelling out that in virtue of God's blessing the servant's house shall be blessed for ever; Ellicott compares the certainty of a blessing that cannot be reversed to Balaam's word in Numbers 22:6, that he whom God blesses is blessed indeed.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reaches the same assurance through the Targum: 'Thou beginnest to bless the house of thy servant, therefore it shall be blessed for ever.' John Wesley marks the fitness of the ending — David's prayer 'concludes, as God's promise did,' with 'for ever,' looking back to the everlasting throne pledged earlier in the chapter; 'God's word looks at things eternal, and so should our desires and hopes.'

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in the last clause the very language of triumphant faith. In Samuel the prayer ends with holy desire — 'let the house of thy servant be blessed'; but in Chronicles it ends with holy assurance — 'thou blessest, and it shall be blessed for ever.' Men can but beg the blessing, Henry says; it is God that commands it, and what he designs he effects, what he promises he performs, for saying and doing are not two things with him. His blessings cannot be revoked or opposed, and their benefits outlast time itself. The witnesses do not divide; they close the chapter as David closed his prayer, resting the eternal security of his house not on the strength of the dynasty but on the unchangeable blessing of God — the blessing that, once pronounced, is blessed for ever, and whose final and unbreakable object is the everlasting kingdom of David's greater Son.
